name: Rooibos tea, brewed
longCovidBenefits: Uniquely rich in aspalathin and nothofagin — rare antioxidants found almost exclusively in rooibos — which help reduce oxidative stress linked to mitochondrial dysfunction and post-exertional malaise in Long COVID.
Completely caffeine-free, making it safe for Long COVID patients with autonomic dysregulation, POTS, or sleep disturbances who need to avoid stimulants.
Contains quercetin and luteolin derivatives that support mast cell stabilisation, providing a warm beverage option that is genuinely safe for MCAS patients.
longCovidCautions: Negligible caloric and micronutrient content means rooibos cannot replace nutritive beverages; use as a supplementary hydration strategy rather than a dietary staple.
Some individuals report mild estrogenic activity from rooibos flavonoids; those with hormone-sensitive conditions should moderate intake and consult a clinician.
Commercial rooibos blends may include additives, flavorings, or dried fruit pieces that introduce histamine or FODMAP load; choose plain unflavored varieties.

longCovidServings.name: Small Soothing Cup
longCovidServings.timing: morning or before bed
longCovidServings.reason: Half-cup portion allows tolerance testing and provides a caffeine-free warm ritual that may help regulate the autonomic nervous system without overstimulating.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: Half a standard mug

longCovidServings.name: Standard Recovery Cup
longCovidServings.timing: between meals or as an afternoon drink
longCovidServings.reason: A full cup delivers meaningful antioxidant exposure from aspalathin and nothofagin while contributing to daily hydration goals, important for POTS and fatigue management.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: One standard mug or teacup

longCovidServings.name: Generous Daily Portion
longCovidServings.timing: spread across the day
longCovidServings.reason: Two cups spread across the day provides consistent polyphenol exposure to support anti-inflammatory pathways; safe even at this volume given zero caffeine and very low FODMAP load.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: A large travel mug or two standard cups

ai_metadata_assessment.notes: Rooibos tea (brewed) is virtually calorie-free and nutrient-sparse per 100ml, but its clinical value for Long COVID lies entirely in its unique polyphenol profile. Aspalathin is a C-glucosyl dihydrochalcone not found in any other common food or beverage; it has demonstrated antioxidant, anti-diabetic, and cardioprotective activity in vitro. Nothofagin adds additional antioxidant coverage. Zero caffeine makes rooibos uniquely suitable for autonomic dysregulation and sleep-disturbed Long COVID patients who miss the ritual of hot beverages. Low oxalate, low histamine, MCAS-safe, and certified low-FODMAP. No meaningful macronutrient, vitamin, or mineral contribution at typical serving volumes. Best positioned as a therapeutic beverage supplement rather than a nutritional source.

notes: Uniquely caffeine-free; aspalathin and nothofagin are MCAS-stabilising antioxidants not found elsewhere

name: Zucchini, pan-fried
longCovidBenefits: Provides a moderate caloric boost (80 kcal/100g) compared to raw zucchini, useful for Long COVID patients with poor appetite who need energy-dense but easily digested vegetables.
Retains meaningful lutein and zeaxanthin (~2125 mcg) even after frying, supporting visual health and reducing neuroinflammation relevant to brain fog.
The added oil increases fat-soluble vitamin absorption (beta-carotene, vitamin E, vitamin K), improving overall nutrient bioavailability from the meal.
longCovidCautions: Pan-frying in industrial seed oils increases the omega-6 load and risk of oxidative byproducts (aldehydes) when oil is overheated; use olive oil or avocado oil at moderate heat.
Higher fat content compared to other zucchini preparations may slow gastric emptying, potentially worsening nausea or bloating in patients with gastroparesis or post-COVID digestive issues.
Caloric density (~80 kcal vs. ~17 kcal steamed) may require adjustment of portion size for patients managing weight or blood sugar during Long COVID recovery.

longCovidServings.name: Small Side Portion
longCovidServings.timing: with lunch or dinner
longCovidServings.reason: Smaller serving limits fat load while providing vegetable variety; suitable for patients with nausea or reduced digestive tolerance.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: About 4-5 thin slices or a small handful

longCovidServings.name: Standard Recovery Portion
longCovidServings.timing: main meal side dish
longCovidServings.reason: Provides a practical vegetable serving with boosted caloric density to support energy needs without overwhelming digestion.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: A medium side portion, roughly the size of your open palm

longCovidServings.name: Larger Portion
longCovidServings.timing: as a main vegetable dish or light meal base
longCovidServings.reason: Suitable for patients with improved tolerance and higher caloric needs; pairs well with protein sources to form a complete recovery meal.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: A generous restaurant-style side portion, about 1.5 open palms

ai_metadata_assessment.notes: Pan-fried zucchini retains the low-FODMAP, low-histamine, MCAS-safe properties of raw zucchini while gaining caloric density from absorbed oil. The fat increase (from ~0.3g to ~5.3g per 100g) enhances fat-soluble carotenoid absorption, particularly lutein/zeaxanthin which is notably well preserved through frying. Vitamin C loss (~40%) is expected from heat. Net Long COVID benefit depends heavily on choice of frying oil; olive or avocado oil recommended. Protein contribution remains modest. Primary benefit is palatability and caloric support for patients with appetite loss.

notes: Use olive oil at moderate heat; avoid overheating oil to minimise aldehyde formation

name: Zucchini, steamed
longCovidBenefits: Among the most gentle vegetables for compromised digestive systems — very low FODMAP, low fiber load, and high water content (94.8%) make steamed zucchini ideal for Long COVID patients with post-COVID gut symptoms.
Retains excellent lutein and zeaxanthin levels (~2125 mcg per 100g), antioxidants that cross the blood-brain barrier and may reduce neuroinflammation contributing to brain fog.
Low caloric density (17 kcal/100g) allows generous portions that support satiety and micronutrient intake without triggering post-exertional metabolic stress or blood glucose spikes.
longCovidCautions: Very low protein (1.1g) and calorie content mean steamed zucchini should be paired with protein and healthy fat sources to form a nutritionally complete Long COVID recovery meal.
Steaming causes moderate vitamin C loss (approximately 30% vs. raw); patients relying on zucchini for vitamin C intake should supplement with raw vegetables where tolerated.

The soft texture after steaming may reduce chewing effort but also reduces the gut motility stimulus from mechanical food processing; patients with constipation may benefit from leaving slight firmness.

longCovidServings.name: Introductory Gentle Portion
longCovidServings.timing: with any meal
longCovidServings.reason: Small serving for patients in early reintroduction phases or with significant gut sensitivity; virtually no FODMAP or histamine burden.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: About 4-5 slices or a small side portion

longCovidServings.name: Standard Recovery Portion
longCovidServings.timing: lunch or dinner side dish
longCovidServings.reason: Moderate serving provides meaningful potassium, folate, and lutein/zeaxanthin while remaining very light on digestion.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: About half a medium zucchini, sliced

longCovidServings.name: Generous Vegetable Base
longCovidServings.timing: as a main vegetable component of a meal
longCovidServings.reason: Larger portion (still only 34 kcal) is suitable for patients who need volume to feel satisfied without energy cost; excellent base for adding protein toppings.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: One full medium zucchini, steamed and sliced

ai_metadata_assessment.notes: Steamed zucchini is the gentlest preparation for Long COVID patients. Nutrient retention is superior to boiling (no water-soluble nutrient leaching) and avoids the added fat and oxidative risk of frying. Key highlights: very low FODMAP (confirmed safe at 65g per Monash University), high lutein/zeaxanthin (2125 mcg — a meaningful dose for neuroprotection), excellent potassium-to-sodium ratio (93:1) supporting dysautonomia management. Protein and caloric contribution are too low for standalone nutrition but as a vegetable component it is nearly ideal for MCAS, ME/CFS, and POTS patients.

notes: Best Long COVID preparation of zucchini; zero FODMAP/histamine burden, maximum gentle nutrition

name: Zucchini, roasted
longCovidBenefits: Roasting concentrates nutrients through mild water loss, increasing potassium (340mg/100g) and carotenoid density relative to raw zucchini, supporting electrolyte balance important for POTS.
The Maillard reaction produces mild browning compounds that enhance flavour and palatability, helping Long COVID patients with taste alterations or poor appetite maintain dietary variety.
Very low FODMAP and histamine burden even after dry-heat roasting makes this a safe, flavourful vegetable option for patients following MCAS or low-histamine elimination diets.
longCovidCautions: Roasting at temperatures above 200°C may generate acrylamide in the browned surfaces; roast at 180-190°C to balance flavour development with reduced formation of potentially inflammatory compounds.
Any added oil increases caloric density and fat load; use minimal olive oil (1 tsp per serving) and avoid high-smoke-point refined oils that may degrade under oven heat.
Over-roasting until very soft may cause significant folate degradation; aim for tender-crisp texture (15-20 min at 190°C) to preserve water-soluble vitamins.

longCovidServings.name: Small Side
longCovidServings.timing: with any meal
longCovidServings.reason: Conservative serving for patients with appetite suppression or early-phase recovery; flavour is more concentrated than steamed so a smaller amount still feels satisfying.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: About 5-6 roasted spears or rounds

longCovidServings.name: Standard Portion
longCovidServings.timing: lunch or dinner
longCovidServings.reason: Provides a satisfying vegetable side with concentrated flavour and meaningful potassium and lutein/zeaxanthin without significant caloric load.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: A medium side bowl of roasted zucchini spears

longCovidServings.name: Generous Vegetable Portion
longCovidServings.timing: as main vegetable base of a meal
longCovidServings.reason: Large portions remain low-calorie (~56 kcal) and high-volume, supporting satiety and micronutrient intake; excellent base for sheet-pan meals with protein.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: A large bowl or sheet pan portion, about 1 full medium zucchini

ai_metadata_assessment.notes: Roasted zucchini offers a palatability advantage over steamed for Long COVID patients experiencing taste changes. Moderate water loss (~5%) mildly concentrates minerals, especially potassium (340mg/100g vs 280mg steamed), supporting dysautonomia/POTS management. Lutein/zeaxanthin well preserved under dry heat. Histamine remains very low as dry roasting without adding aged/fermented ingredients does not trigger significant histamine formation. A minimal spray of olive oil is recommended to aid fat-soluble carotenoid absorption. Overall profile is very similar to steamed but with superior palatability characteristics.

notes: Roast at 180-190°C; minimal olive oil; excellent for taste-altered patients who find steamed vegetables unpalatable

name: Shallots, raw
longCovidBenefits: Exceptionally high quercetin content (among the highest of common culinary vegetables) supports mast cell stabilisation, reduces histamine release, and has demonstrated antiviral activity relevant to Long COVID viral persistence.

Contains allicin precursors (alliin + alliinase) and organosulphur compounds that boost glutathione synthesis — the body's primary antioxidant — directly addressing mitochondrial oxidative stress central to Long COVID fatigue.

Vitamin B6 content (0.345mg/100g) supports neurological function, serotonin and dopamine synthesis, and energy metabolism, potentially benefiting brain fog and mood disturbances in Long COVID patients.
longCovidCautions: Shallots are HIGH FODMAP due to fructans; even small quantities (>15g raw) may trigger IBS symptoms, bloating, or abdominal pain in Long COVID patients with concurrent gut dysbiosis or SIBO.
Organosulphur compounds may act as mild mast cell triggers in highly sensitised MCAS patients; monitor individual tolerance and introduce in small cooked portions rather than raw.

Raw shallots have a more intense FODMAP impact than cooked; cooking breaks down some fructans and reduces the gut burden — patients with FODMAP sensitivity should use cooked shallots only, in quantities under 15g.

typical_serving.description: 15g (1 small shallot or as a garnish)

longCovidServings.name: FODMAP-Safe Garnish
longCovidServings.timing: with any meal, raw or cooked
longCovidServings.reason: Threshold portion below Monash University's FODMAP trigger level for shallots; delivers flavour and a meaningful quercetin dose without fructan overload.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: One small shallot bulb, or about a tablespoon minced

longCovidServings.name: Cooked Moderate Portion
longCovidServings.timing: cooked into dishes (soups, sautés) at lunch or dinner
longCovidServings.reason: Cooking reduces fructan content; 30g cooked is tolerated by many with mild FODMAP sensitivity and provides substantial quercetin and organosulphur benefits.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: Two small shallots or one medium shallot, cooked

longCovidServings.name: Full Serving (FODMAP-tolerant only)
longCovidServings.timing: cooked into a main dish
longCovidServings.reason: Suitable only for Long COVID patients who have confirmed tolerance to fructans; delivers a clinically meaningful quercetin dose (~30-60mg) for anti-inflammatory and mast cell stabilising effects.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: Three to four small shallots, cooked into a dish

ai_metadata_assessment.notes: Shallots represent a high-value quercetin source (estimated 33-60mg/100g, among the richest culinary sources alongside red onion and capers) with excellent organosulphur and glutathione-boosting potential. However, their HIGH FODMAP classification (fructans) creates a significant tension for Long COVID patients with GI symptoms. The key recommendation is cooking to break down fructans and limiting portions to ≤15g raw or ≤30g cooked. For MCAS, organosulphur compounds are generally well tolerated unlike histamine-liberating foods, but individual sensitivity varies. safeForMCAS set to false as a conservative default; clinically, cooked shallots in small amounts are often well tolerated. The quercetin content alone makes shallots one of the most pharmacologically interesting flavouring vegetables for Long COVID, warranting tolerance testing.

notes: HIGH FODMAP — limit to 15g raw or 30g cooked; highest quercetin in common culinary vegetables; cook before using for FODMAP-sensitive patients

name: Ginger root, raw
longCovidBenefits: Gingerols and shogaols inhibit NF-κB and COX-2 inflammatory pathways, providing anti-inflammatory activity comparable to low-dose NSAIDs without the GI side effects — directly relevant to systemic inflammation in Long COVID.

Clinically proven to reduce nausea, gastroparesis symptoms, and gastric motility dysfunction — highly beneficial for the post-COVID autonomic gut symptoms affecting many patients, including nausea triggered by POTS.

Contains 6-gingerol and 6-paradol which have demonstrated antiviral activity against multiple respiratory viruses in vitro, potentially supporting immune clearance relevant to Long COVID viral persistence hypotheses.
longCovidCautions: Ginger is a mild COX-1 inhibitor and may slightly thin the blood; patients taking anticoagulants (warfarin, aspirin) or those with clotting disorders should use culinary amounts only and consult their clinician.

Raw ginger is classified as low-FODMAP at typical culinary doses (up to 5g/serving per Monash University), but larger amounts or ginger supplements may provoke digestive discomfort in sensitive individuals.

Some patients with MCAS report sensitivity to the pungent compounds (gingerols) in raw ginger; starting with small cooked amounts and monitoring for reactions is advisable before using raw ginger freely.

longCovidServings.name: Flavouring Dose
longCovidServings.timing: added to cooking or tea, any time of day
longCovidServings.reason: Minimal amount sufficient to introduce gingerols into the meal and support anti-nausea effects; ideal starting dose for MCAS patients assessing individual tolerance.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: Half a teaspoon of freshly grated ginger — about the size of a small pea

longCovidServings.name: Therapeutic Culinary Dose
longCovidServings.timing: in cooking, smoothies, or ginger tea — twice daily
longCovidServings.reason: Provides clinically meaningful gingerol intake for anti-nausea, anti-inflammatory, and gut motility benefits without exceeding low-FODMAP thresholds.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: A thumb-tip sized knob of fresh ginger, finely grated

longCovidServings.name: High Functional Dose
longCovidServings.timing: in a meal or ginger tea, once daily
longCovidServings.reason: Higher dose for patients specifically targeting nausea, gastroparesis, or systemic inflammation; remains low-FODMAP but may require tolerance building for unaccustomed digestive systems.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: A 2cm piece of ginger root, grated — roughly the size of your thumb from knuckle to tip

ai_metadata_assessment.notes: Fresh ginger root is one of the highest-priority functional foods for Long COVID. Its bioactive gingerols (6-, 8-, 10-gingerol) and shogaols (formed on heating/drying) have robust evidence for anti-inflammatory, anti-nausea, pro-motility, and antiviral actions. Magnesium content (43mg/100g) is notably high for a root vegetable, supporting mitochondrial ATP synthesis. Potassium (415mg) supports POTS management. Choline (28.8mg) supports neurological function. Low-FODMAP at culinary doses. MCAS-safe in culinary amounts for most patients though individual sensitivity to pungent phenols exists. Strongly recommended as a daily culinary staple for Long COVID — arguably one of the most evidence-backed anti-inflammatory foods accessible as a whole food.

notes: One of the most evidence-backed anti-inflammatory whole foods for Long COVID; low FODMAP at culinary doses; anti-nausea, pro-motility, antiviral

name: Turmeric root, raw
longCovidBenefits: Curcumin — the principal curcuminoid in turmeric — is among the most clinically studied natural anti-inflammatory compounds, inhibiting NF-κB, TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β simultaneously, targeting the cytokine pathways implicated in Long COVID systemic inflammation.

Exceptionally high magnesium content (193mg/100g — among the highest of any commonly used food) supports mitochondrial ATP production, nerve signal transmission, and muscle relaxation, directly addressing energy production deficits in ME/CFS and Long COVID.

Curcumin has demonstrated neuroprotective activity by crossing the blood-brain barrier, reducing neuroinflammation, and supporting BDNF (brain-derived neurotrophic factor) expression, potentially ameliorating brain fog and cognitive dysfunction.
longCovidCautions: Curcumin has very low oral bioavailability (~1%) from raw turmeric alone; pairing with black pepper (piperine) increases absorption by up to 2000%, and consuming with a fat-containing meal further enhances uptake — always use with pepper and fat for therapeutic benefit.

High doses from supplements (not culinary amounts) may interact with anticoagulants, antiplatelet drugs, and certain chemotherapy agents; culinary use of fresh turmeric root (5-15g/day) is safe for most patients.
Turmeric contains moderate oxalates; patients with a history of kidney stones (calcium oxalate type) should moderate intake and ensure adequate hydration.

longCovidServings.name: Flavouring Dose
longCovidServings.timing: in cooking, golden milk, or smoothies — daily
longCovidServings.reason: Introductory amount for patients new to fresh turmeric; delivers flavour and colour with minimal curcumin but allows the digestive system to adjust.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: A pea-sized to fingernail-sized piece of fresh turmeric root, grated

longCovidServings.name: Therapeutic Culinary Dose
longCovidServings.timing: in a meal or golden milk with black pepper and fat — once or twice daily
longCovidServings.reason: Provides a meaningful curcuminoid dose (~30-50mg curcumin) in culinary form; pair with a pinch of black pepper and olive oil to raise bioavailability substantially.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: A 1.5cm piece of fresh turmeric root, grated — about the width of a finger

longCovidServings.name: High Functional Dose
longCovidServings.timing: in a main meal, once daily, always with fat and black pepper
longCovidServings.reason: Maximises curcumin delivery through whole-food form without the bioavailability and interaction risks of high-dose supplements; appropriate for Long COVID patients with confirmed inflammatory load.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: A 2cm knob of fresh turmeric root, fully grated

ai_metadata_assessment.notes: Fresh turmeric root is arguably the single most pharmacologically relevant functional food for Long COVID inflammation. Its curcuminoid content (curcumin, demethoxycurcumin, bisdemethoxycurcumin) simultaneously targets NF-κB, NLRP3 inflammasome, and JAK-STAT pathways — the precise inflammatory cascades implicated in Long COVID. The extraordinary magnesium content (193mg/100g at culinary amounts used daily — 10-15g — delivers 20-30mg Mg, a meaningful contribution to daily targets). Iron (3.1mg/100g) supports oxygen-carrying capacity relevant to fatigue. The critical limitation is bioavailability: without piperine (black pepper) and dietary fat, curcumin absorption is negligible. App should flag this pairing recommendation prominently. Low-FODMAP, MCAS-safe, low histamine. Oxalates are moderate — not a concern at culinary doses unless patient has active oxalate kidney stones.

notes: Most anti-inflammatory whole food for Long COVID; MUST pair with black pepper + fat for bioavailability; do not confuse with turmeric powder (concentrated, different dose)

name: Green onions (scallions), raw
longCovidBenefits: Outstanding vitamin K content (207mcg/100g — over 170% of daily adequate intake) supports endothelial health and coagulation regulation, relevant to the microclotting and vascular dysfunction hypotheses in Long COVID.

Exceptional folate density (64mcg/100g) supports methylation pathways, DNA repair, and neurotransmitter synthesis — key processes impaired in Long COVID patients with neurological symptoms and fatigue.

Low-FODMAP (the green tops are safe even for FODMAP-sensitive patients, unlike the bulb which contains some fructans), making green onions a safe shallot substitute that still provides meaningful quercetin and organosulphur compounds.
longCovidCautions: The white bulb end of green onions contains more fructans than the green tops; FODMAP-sensitive patients should use only the green portion and discard the white base above the root.

Vitamin K content is very high (207mcg/100g); patients on warfarin anticoagulation therapy must maintain consistent daily vitamin K intake and should consult their clinician before significantly increasing green onion consumption.
Raw green onions may cause mild digestive discomfort (bloating, gas) from organosulphur compounds in patients with severe gut dysbiosis; lightly cooking reduces this while preserving most nutrients.

typical_serving.description: 15g (2-3 stalks, chopped — green tops only for FODMAP-safe use)

longCovidServings.name: Garnish Dose
longCovidServings.timing: as garnish on any meal
longCovidServings.reason: Small garnish portion still delivers meaningful vitamin K and folate in proportion; zero FODMAP burden from green tops alone.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: 1-2 thinly sliced scallion stalks scattered over a dish

longCovidServings.name: Standard Serving
longCovidServings.timing: in salads, soups, stir-fries, or as garnish — daily
longCovidServings.reason: Provides a clinically meaningful folate (19mcg) and vitamin K (62mcg) contribution, plus quercetin and organosulphur compounds; low FODMAP if using green portions.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: A small bunch of chopped green onion tops, about 2 tablespoons

longCovidServings.name: Generous Portion
longCovidServings.timing: in a main meal — lunch or dinner
longCovidServings.reason: Larger serving for patients without FODMAP sensitivity who want to maximise vitamin K (124mcg), folate (38mcg), and quercetin intake from this accessible whole-food source.
longCovidServings.visualEquivalent: A generous handful of chopped scallions, about 4 tablespoons

ai_metadata_assessment.notes: Green onions (scallions) are nutritionally superior to their low-key culinary status suggests. Key highlights: vitamin K (207mcg/100g — exceptional), folate (64mcg — excellent for a garnish vegetable), calcium (72mg — one of the better non-dairy plant sources), iron (1.48mg), and vitamin C (18.8mg) supporting iron absorption in the same food. Lutein/zeaxanthin (1137mcg) supports neuroprotection. Importantly, the green leafy tops are low-FODMAP even though the white bulb is moderate-FODMAP — this makes green onions the ideal safe replacement for shallots and onions in FODMAP-sensitive Long COVID patients. Organosulphur content is lower than shallots but still contributes to glutathione synthesis. MCAS-safe, low-histamine, excellent all-round safety profile. Recommended as a daily staple for Long COVID given its nutrient density, ubiquity, low cost, and suitability across all dietary restriction patterns encountered in this patient population.

notes: Excellent FODMAP-safe shallot substitute; use green tops only for strict FODMAP compliance; outstanding vitamin K and folate source
